pages.title: https://www.molygraph.com/company
pages.text:
We are a diverse group of passionate researchers, scientists, technologists, engineers, and designers, dedicated to the future of oil and lubrication.

With deep expertise, a collaborative approach, and a commitment to innovation in the field of oil and lubrication, we constantly explore new products and push the boundaries of what is possible.

Our legacy is built on the firm foundation of the Anand Engineers, which was laid in 1976 by Mr. Jayantilal Shah with a great entrepreneurial spirit. The name “Anand” was adopted from Swami Vivekanand, whose exemplary life continues to inspire and motivate all of us, even today.

Learn More About Our History

Who we are

Molygraph Lubricants is a leader in the innovation, formulation, and manufacturer of super specialty lubricants. Our proven experience in product development and deep technical knowledge on the application side, allows us to add value to our customer's processes. Our customer profile ranges from Automotive OEM & first-tier suppliers, the world's largest steel & sponge plants, major hot forging/HVAC/metal forming manufacturers, prominent suppliers in the oil & gas industry as well as top-of-the-line manufacturers in various heavy and general engineering segments.

Learn More About Our Process

Meet our leadership team

The leadership team comprises technical experts and thought leaders with the single-minded objective of helping our customers grow.

Our team fosters an environment of innovation and continuous improvement, encouraging open communication and teamwork among all employees of the organization. Through their technical expertise, strategic thinking, and unwavering commitment to customer success.

Meet Molygraph’s Senior Management

Innovation, Research and Development

The Molygraph Lubricants innovation and technology center is where we develop the best products for our customers. The labs consist of analytical and tribological instruments in addition to standard ASTM test rigs. Our R&D team works in close collaboration with our customers to ensure an optimal solution for their products. This approach has helped us to achieve success across industries and create a unique portfolio of products.

Learn More About Our Process

pages.title: Molygraph Company History | Pioneering Excellence in Lubrication Solutions
pages.text:
Molygraph Company History | Pioneering Excellence in Lubrication Solutions

1976

Founded

1987

Introduction of Molygraph Lubricants

1999

Beginning of a growth story

2003

Dominance in the Sponge Iron Sector

2010

Complete product portfolio in Forging, Metalworking Industry and Food & Pharma

2011

New Plant and R&D Setup

2014

Molygraph Goes Global

2015

Introduction of Calcium Sulfonate and Polyurea Greases

2020

DSIR Accreditation for R&D Center

2023

Achieved Triple ISO Certifications

1976:Founded

The foundation of the Anand Engineers was laid in 1976 by Mr. Jayantilal Shah with a great entrepreneurial spirit. The name “Anand” was adopted from Swami Vivekanand, whose exemplary life inspired and motivated Jayantibhai and in turn all of us.

1987:Introduction of Molygraph Lubricants

With a business built primarily on being a leading engineering and chemical equipment manufacturers, Molygraph Greases and Sprays were introduced as a business diversification strategy.

1999:Beginning of a growth story

There was a clear focus and vision of developing Molygraph Lubricants into a world-class specialty lubricants brand. The introduction of market and application-driven products helped Molygraph gain market share rapidly.

2003:Dominance in the Sponge Iron Sector

Innovative new products in the Sponge Iron Slip-Seal applications allowed Molygraph to gain a major market share and catapulted us into one of the largest specialty lubricant manufacturers in India.

2010:Complete product portfolio in Forging, Metalworking Industry and Food & Pharma

Graphol® series of forging lubricants, SmoothKut® series of cutting oils, SmoothForm® of metal forming lubricants, & Safol® series of food grade lubricants.

2011:New Plant and R&D Setup

A new state-of-the-art plant and R&D center was set up to cater to the ever-growing demands of the business. This has increased our manufacturing capacities five-fold.

2014:Molygraph Goes Global

We started the International Business division with a core focus on Asia, Middle East & Africa. Today we proudly export to 15+ countries.

2015:Introduction of Calcium Sulfonate and Polyurea Greases

Standardization of high-performance greases like Polyurea and Calcium Sulfonate. The product portfolio comes full circle to include the entire range of grease chemistries.

2020:DSIR Accreditation for R&D Center

Our Research and Development laboratory received accreditation from the Department of Scientific and Industrial Research and the Ministry of Science and Technology, validating our commitment to scientific advancement and innovation.

2023:Achieved Triple ISO Certifications

Our organization officially certified in ISO 9001, ISO 14001, ISO 45001 standards reflecting our unwavering commitment towards customers, partners, and the environment.

pages.title: Our Efficient Manufacturing Process for Quality Lubrication Solutions | Molygraph
pages.text:
Our Efficient Manufacturing Process for Quality Lubrication Solutions | Molygraph

Steps to the right solution

Every customer's situation is unique. By first determining the customer's situation, we set the foundations for successful cooperation.

Survey and Analysis

Lubricant Optimization

Implementation and Monitoring

Prompt Delivery and Support

Value added Services

Survey and Analysis

Our team conducts a thorough survey and assessment of your industrial lubrication system, identifying any existing or potential issues and gathering necessary data for analysis. We evaluate the overall performance to provide you with a clear understanding of the system's current state.

Lubricant Optimization

Based on the survey findings, we develop a customized lubricant optimization plan. This involves recommending the most suitable lubricant types, grades, and quantities to enhance efficiency, reduce friction, and improve equipment lifespan. We work closely with you to ensure that the plan meets your unique requirements and focuses on optimal value creation.

Implementation and Monitoring

Once the lubricant optimization plan is approved, we proceed with the implementation phase. Our experienced technicians execute the necessary lubricant changes, monitor the system closely, and ensure a smooth transition without any disruptions to your operations. We provide comprehensive documentation and reporting throughout the process to keep you informed.

Prompt Delivery and Support

We understand the importance of timely delivery to keep your operations running smoothly. Our dedicated logistics team ensures the prompt delivery of lubricants, eliminating any delays or downtime. Additionally, our customer support team is available to address any concerns or queries you may have during and after the delivery process. With our commitment to excellent service and support, you can rely on us to provide timely delivery and reliable logistics solutions for your lubricant needs.

Value added Services

At Molygraph, we also provide a range of professional services to support our clients in optimizing their operations and achieving peak performance. Withconsultancyservices, we address specific challenges and enhance efficiency for our clients. We also offerlubrication auditsto assess current practices and identify opportunities for improvement. We specialize inmaintenance planning, training programs, on-site support, lubricant analysis, coolant management program, and grease testingto assess the lubricant condition and performance, facilitating informed decision-making. With our comprehensive services, we are committed to delivering specialized solutions that drive efficiency, reliability, and sustainability for our valued clients.

pages.text:
Privacy Policy | Molygraph Lubricants

Privacy Policy

Anand Engineers Pvt Ltd, referred to as Molygraph Lubricants hereinafter, is the owner of the copyright of this website and its contents unless otherwise stated. We are committed to protecting the privacy of the users of this website. The content of this website is intended for general information purposes and is not a substitute for specialist recommendations. It does not constitute a guarantee or assurance of the product properties, neither expressly nor by implication. It is our endeavor to keep this site updated; however, we do not guarantee the latest updates, correctness, and completeness of the data presented herewith. We accept no liability for damages arising from the use of the information available on this site. The Privacy and Data Protection Policy of Molygraph is stated here. We have provided a hyperlink to third-party websites in good faith that the content and policy of the hyperlinked website are correct. However, other websites may have different privacy policies. We strongly recommend that you read the privacy statement of each such website to find out how your personal information is being protected/used. We, therefore, accept no liability for damages.

Molygraph Lubricants will collect only personally identifiable information (such as Name, Company Name, Telephone Number, Email Address, etc.) that you voluntarily provide on our website. We may collect other information of a generalized nature (such as the pages you have accessed, information you have downloaded/requested, etc.) to improve our website and realign the format in which the documents/information are presented to meet your requirement for easy navigation and accessibility. The information you have voluntarily provided will be used to respond to your inquiries and offer you the requested information.

By providing your personal details on this website, you signify that you have allowed us to use the same to respond to you. We shall also be permitted to send information about our products, newsletters, etc. to you as and when the opportunity arises. We will not use and/or share the information provided by you for direct marketing purposes with any third party. However, if necessary and required, we shall use and disclose the personal and other information provided by you in law enforcement investigations to the extent required.

Presently or in the future, cookies may be used on our website, which may then be stored on your hard drive so we can recognize you when you return. You may set your browser to notify you when you receive a cookie. Registering with Molygraph online signifies your unconditional consent to receive such cookies. This information facilitates your use of our website and ensures that you do not need to re-enter your details every time you visit us. You can erase or choose to block this information from your computer if you want to (please refer to your browser settings to do so). You assume full responsibility for the protection of your computer system, including computer hardware and software, stored data on your computer system, and the stored data and computer systems (including hardware and software) of third parties who may access or be otherwise connected to your computer system. You will assume the responsibility of ensuring that programs or other data downloaded or otherwise received from this website are free from viruses, worms, Trojan horses, or other items of a destructive nature.

Molygraph Lubricants reserves the right to modify this privacy policy at any time. Any such modifications will be immediately reflected on this page.

Molygraph

Business Development Manager – Export Sales (Industrial Lubricants)

Job Location:

Vietnam, Indonesia, Thailand, Saudi

Qualification:

B.E. (Chemical or Mechanical) / B.Sc. (Chemistry) / M.Sc. (Chemistry) or any relevant Engineering degree, along with MBA or PGDM in Sales or Marketing.

Experience:

6–8 years of experience in Industrial Lubricants or Cutting Tools.

Job Responsibility

Identify and develop new export markets for industrial lubricants, metalworking fluids, and specialty industrial greases.

Achieve annual export sales targets in value, and contribution margin.

Build and strengthen long-term relationships with industrial customers

Act as a bridge between customers and internal technical teams for product selection and application support.

Coordinate with manufacturing, supply chain, QA, and logistics teams to ensure timely execution of export orders.

Represent the company at international trade fairs, technical seminars, and industry exhibitions.

Key Skills

Strong commercial and negotiation skills

Analytical, self-driven, and result-oriented approach

Excellent communication and cross-cultural management skills

pages.title: Molygraph
pages.text:
Molygraph

Tribologist

Job Location:

Palghar

Qualification:

Master’s or Ph.D. in Mechanical Engineering, Chemical Engineering, Materials Science, or a related discipline, with specialization or strong academic focus in Tribology.

Experience:

4–5 years of experience in Tribology, Lubricant, or Bearing Application Engineering.

Job Responsibility

Analyze and interpret data from tribology experiments and real-world applications to understand wear mechanisms and lubricant performance.

Collaborate with cross-functional teams including chemists, engineers, and product managers to develop new products and improve existing ones.

Provide technical expertise and support to customers, including troubleshooting and recommending lubrication solutions for specific applications.

Stay abreast of the latest advancements in tribology and related fields, and apply this knowledge to product development.

Design and conduct experiments under simulated service conditions to evaluate the performance of lubricants under various environmental and operational parameters.

Operate and maintain specific tribological instrumentation for testing wear and load performance of lubricants, such as pin-on-disk, four-ball testers, and rheometers.

Ensure all activities are conducted in compliance with safety, regulatory, and quality standards.

Key Skills

Strong understanding of the principles of tribology, lubrication, and material science.

Experimental design and statistical data analysis.

Excellent problem-solving skills and the ability to think creatively to overcome technical challenges.

Strong communication skills, both written and verbal, with the ability to present complex technical information clearly and persuasively.

Molygraph

Territory Sales Manager- Furniture Fitting

Job Location:

Rajkot / Ahmedabad

Qualification:

B.E. (Chemical or Mechanical) / B.Sc. (Chemistry) / M.Sc. (Chemistry) or any relevant Engineering degree, along with MBA or PGDM in Sales or Marketing.

Experience:

5–10 years of experience in Lubricants or Technical Sales.

Job Responsibility

Responsible for the sales to designated partners and secondary customers and monitoring.

Achieves the revenue and profitability quotas.

To identify and propose new sales partners as required.

To achieve collection targets for payments and other statutory forms.

Assisting channel to achieve price increase from secondary customers as required by the company.

To explore new business opportunity & segments within the designated areas.

To monitor activates and strategies of competitors and formulate strategies to counter them.

Responsible for tracking customer information, pipelines, forecasts and reports.

To periodically review secondary sales reports and inventory levels of sales partners.

Lead the Sales partners Representatives and Specialists, inclusive of managing performance, coaching and mentoring.

Constructive problem solving, facilitating creative improvements.

Sharing technical and sales experiences with rest of the sales team to enhance team productivity.

Identify development potential in secondary accounts by studying current business, interviewing customer personnel identifying and evaluating additional needs, analyzing opportunities.

Initiates sales process by building relationships, qualifying potential and scheduling appointments.

To act as a single window for handling sales partners and their customer issues and grievances.

Making and submitting proposals, showing customers the benefit of using the company’s product.

Planning conducting and monitoring trials at customer end.

Negotiating prices with secondary customers to support channel partners margins expectations.

To meet regularly all of sales partners and secondary customers to gauge their satisfaction.

To communicate all of company’s developments, policies to sales partners and secondary customers.

Ensure proper distribution of gifts and souvenirs as allotted by the company time to time.

Ensuring maximum participation of sales partner and secondary customers personnel in seminars and presentations held by the company.

Develop and conduct one-on-one and group sales presentations, explaining company’s products and services and benefits to customers, introducing new products and services.

To conduct trainings at sales partners and major secondary customer’s locations to spread awareness on best practices on using company’s products.

Establishes an environment and foundation for future sales growth. Sell value to customers by creating better solution to customers and documenting their successes.

Smoothly communicating and Implementing Technological knowhow within the domain.

Working closely with product management team to develop greater value solutions for sales partners and secondary customers.

Evaluate and report market trends and gather competitive information, identify trends that affect current and future growth of sales and profitability.

Key Skills

Strong channel and market understanding.

Good technical product knowledge.

Awareness of competitive products and strategies.

Selling and negotiating skills.

Presentation and communication skills.

Concept and value selling.

pages.title: Lubricants for Automotive Components I Molygraph I Manufacturer In India
pages.text:
Lubricants for Automotive Components I Molygraph I Manufacturer In India

Automotive Components

Molygraph Lubricants makes specialized products for the automotive Industry. These products are developed for exterior, interior, chassis, and electrical components. These products are specifically designed to ensure compliance with all technical and design parameters of the component. OEMs are facing greater demands on in-cabin NVH performance. The AutoDamp series is purpose-built to support these needs, delivering consistent damping behaviour, noise suppression, and a haptic feel.

Client Stories

Learn more on how our products and solutions have helped our customers progress in the Automotive Components.

Molygraph SGH 220 AT successfully increases the lubrication interval upto 175000 km for wheel hub bearings of Heavy Commercial Vehicle.

Read Client Story

AUTO G 35 MV 2 eliminated failures caused due to carbon deposits and copper corrosion in electrical switches

Read Client Story

Core Products

Ultrasyn Premium

In the paper industry, bearing lubrication is critical to ensure smooth operation, prevent premature wear, and extend the lifespan of bearings. Paper mills often operate at high temperatures with water ingress and extreme loads being commonplace. Speciality lubricants for bearing lubrication in the paper industry should have excellent thermal stability, water resistance, and load-carrying capabilities allowing them to maintain their lubricating properties and protect bearings effectively in adverse conditions.

pages.title: Four Grease Tests for Centralized Lubrication Systems | Molygraph
pages.text:
Four Grease Tests for Centralized Lubrication Systems | Molygraph

BLOG

Four Grease Tests for Centralized Lubrication Systems

5 min read | 12 April 2024

When designing a centralized lubrication system, which comes first: the system or the grease selection? While many start by designing the system and then selecting a suitable grease, the optimal approach is to first choose a grease that meets the machine’s specific needs and then design the lubrication system around that lubricant.

Understanding Grease Composition

To begin, let’s clarify what grease is. According to ASTM D288, grease is defined as “a solid-to-semifluid product of dispersion of a thickening agent in a liquid lubricant, with other ingredients added to impart special properties.”

Grease essentially consists of three components:

Base Oil: Making up 70% to 95% of the grease, the base oil is the primary lubricant.

Additives: Comprising 0% to 10% of the grease, additives are included to enhance or suppress certain properties of the base oil, particularly for protection during the startup and shutdown phases.

Thickener: The thickener, which constitutes 3% to 30% of the grease, acts as a vessel to deliver the base oil and additives to the equipment.

Selecting the Right Grease: Four Essential Tests

Now that you understand the composition of grease, let’s explore four tests that are crucial for determining how the grease will perform in a centralized lubrication system.

1. Apparent Viscosity Test

Viscosity is the most critical property of any lubricant. To determine the required base oil viscosity, you need to identify the optimal viscosity for each component of your system. The apparent viscosity of grease, influenced by the base oil, additives, and thickener, is particularly important in centralized lubrication systems. The ASTM D1092 standard test measures this property and helps predict pressure drops in the system under steady flow and constant temperature, with results reported in centipoise.

2. Cone Penetration Test

To determine the required consistency or NLGI grade of the grease, consider factors like operating temperature, speed factor, bearing type, thickener type, and base oil viscosity. The cone penetration test (ASTM D217) gauges a grease’s consistency, both in storage (undisturbed) and during operation (worked). This test is crucial in centralized systems, where grease may sit in supply lines for extended periods before reaching the component.

3. Structural Stability Test

Assessing the grease’s stability under operating conditions is vital. The ASTM D1831 structural stability test involves using a penetrometer to measure the grease's consistency before and after it is subjected to rolling in a stability device for two hours. This test helps determine how the grease will perform under the intended loads and for how long before it begins to fail.

4. Ventability Test

The final test focuses on matching the grease to the lubrication system’s design and the tubing size. The ventability test helps identify the supply-line diameter required for a specific grease. It also determines whether the grease is suitable for use in a centralized lubrication system, ensuring that grease valves and injectors will function properly. The test pressurizes the grease in a 25-foot coil to 1,800 psi, and the pressure is measured after stabilization.

Designing Your Centralized Lubrication System

Armed with the results of these tests, you can now make an informed decision when designing your centralized lubrication system. Always design the system around the grease rather than compromising on essential grease properties just to fit the system. Avoid common mistakes such as using inappropriate supply-line diameters, applying incorrect pressure, or sacrificing crucial grease characteristics. Now, when asked which comes first—the system or the grease—you’ll have the answer.

Share

pages.title: Types Of Friction And Lubrication Regimes - Know about Lubricants, Oils and Greases
pages.text:
Types Of Friction And Lubrication Regimes - Know about Lubricants, Oils and Greases

BLOG

Types Of Friction And Lubrication Regimes

5 min read | 19 April 2023

Friction is a force that opposes the relative motion or tendency of such motion between two surfaces in contact. It plays a crucial role in various mechanical systems and engineering applications. The Stribeck curve helps in understanding friction and lubrication regimes and their importance in designing and optimizing machinery and systems.

Types of Friction:

Static Friction:Static friction is the force that opposes the initiation of motion between two surfaces in contact when they are at rest. It's the friction that must be overcome to set an object in motion. The Stribeck curve's initial portion (Zone I) represents the static friction region.

Kinetic (Dynamic) Friction:Kinetic friction, also known as dynamic friction, occurs when two surfaces are in relative motion. Once an object overcomes static friction and starts moving, it experiences kinetic friction. The flat portion of the Stribeck curve (Zone III) corresponds to the kinetic friction region.

Lubrication Regimes:

Lubrication is the process of reducing friction between two surfaces in relative motion by introducing a lubricant. The type of lubrication regime depends on the conditions and the nature of the lubricant film between the surfaces. There are three main lubrication regimes:

Boundary Lubrication:In this regime, there is minimal or no continuous lubricant film between the surfaces. Instead, the lubricant forms a boundary layer, typically a few molecules thick, which provides some protection against direct metal-to-metal contact. Boundary lubrication is relevant in the mixed friction region (Zone II) of the Stribeck curve.

Mixed Lubrication:Mixed lubrication occurs when there is a combination of boundary and hydrodynamic lubrication. Here, the lubricant film is thicker than in boundary lubrication but still not sufficient to completely separate the surfaces. Mixed lubrication is represented in Zone II of the Stribeck curve, where the friction force decreases as velocity increases.

Hydrodynamic Lubrication:In this regime, a thick and continuous lubricant film completely separates the moving surfaces. This film significantly reduces friction and wear, ensuring smooth and efficient operation. Hydrodynamic lubrication corresponds to the dynamic friction region (Zone III) of the Stribeck curve.

Importance of the Stribeck Curve:

The Stribeck curve is essential in tribology (the study of friction, lubrication, and wear) and mechanical engineering for several reasons:

Design and Optimization:Engineers use the Stribeck curve to understand the frictional behavior of materials and lubricants. This knowledge helps in designing and optimizing systems to minimize friction, reduce wear, and improve efficiency.

Selection of Lubricants:By analyzing where a system operates on the Stribeck curve, engineers can select the appropriate lubricants and lubrication methods to ensure optimal performance and longevity of machinery.

Failure Prevention:Understanding friction and lubrication regimes is critical for preventing component failures due to excessive wear and heat generation, which can lead to costly repairs and downtime.

Energy Efficiency:Minimizing frictional losses is crucial in energy-efficient design. The Stribeck curve aids in identifying conditions where friction can be reduced, leading to energy savings in various applications.

In summary, the Stribeck curve provides valuable insights into the behavior of friction and lubrication regimes, helping engineers and scientists make informed decisions in designing, operating, and maintaining mechanical systems.

Share

pages.title: Chain lubrication - Best Practices For Drives and Conveyors - Know about Lubricants, Oils and Greases
pages.text:
Chain lubrication - Best Practices For Drives and Conveyors - Know about Lubricants, Oils and Greases

BLOG

Chain lubrication – Best Practices For Drives and Conveyors

7 min read | 20 April 2023

We all know that the lubrication of industrial chains is critical. Regular maintenance and lubrication are preconditions for low wear and long service life of the chain drive. The maintenance and lubrication frequency, as well as the related re-lubrication, are determined by the operating conditions of the application.

The right lubricant and optimised lubrication practices increase the life of the chains several times over by reducing wear, increasing corrosion protection and effective noise damping.

Even though many of the low-speed chains run successfully, with little or no oil, routine lubrication on the production line greatly extends the service life of the equipment through multiple ways.

Reduced friction and wear

Energy Efficiency

Enhanced Performance

Noise Reduction

Corrosion Prevention

Temperature Regulation

How are industrial chains lubricated?

Manual Lubrication:

Brush Lubrication: A brush or similar applicator is used to manually apply lubricant to the chain. This method allows for precise control of lubricant application and is often used in applications where over-lubrication must be avoided.

Drip Lubrication: Lubricant is dripped onto the chain at specified intervals. This method is relatively simple and cost-effective but may not provide as consistent lubrication as other methods.

Automatic Lubrication:

Chain Lubrication Systems: Automatic lubrication systems are designed to apply a precise amount of lubricant at regular intervals. These systems can include spray, drip, or brush-type applicators and are often used in high-speed or continuous operation applications.

Centralized Lubrication Systems: In some industrial setups, centralized lubrication systems are used to supply lubricant to multiple points, including chain lubrication points. These systems can be programmed for specific lubrication intervals and quantities

Routine Maintenance and checking for binding

Testing for binding in conveyor chains is important to ensure that the chain operates smoothly and efficiently, as binding can lead to increased wear, reduced productivity, and potential damage to the chain and associated equipment. Here are some methods and steps for testing for binding in conveyor chains:

Visual Inspection:Start with a visual inspection of the entire conveyor chain system. Look for any visible signs of misalignment, wear, damage, or foreign objects that might be causing binding.

Manual Rotation Test:One of the simplest methods is to manually rotate the conveyor chain. If you notice resistance, uneven movement, or areas where the chain seems to be catching or binding, this can indicate a problem.

Load Testing:Apply a load to the conveyor system to simulate real operating conditions. Ensure the load is evenly distributed along the length of the chain. Monitor the chain's movement under load for any signs of binding, such as jerky motion or excessive resistance.

Alignment Check:Verify that all components of the conveyor system, including rollers, sprockets, guides, and the chain itself, are correctly aligned. Misalignment can lead to binding issues.

Tension Check:Ensure that the conveyor chain is properly tensioned. Excessive tension or slack can cause binding. Refer to the manufacturer's guidelines for the recommended tensioning procedure.

Clearance Inspection:Check for adequate clearance between the chain and any nearby equipment or structures. Insufficient clearance can lead to binding as the chain may rub against obstacles.

Pin and Roller Wear:Inspect the chain pins and rollers for wear. Excessive wear can cause the chain to bind as the rollers may not rotate smoothly. Replace any worn components as needed.

Lubrication:Ensure that the conveyor chain is adequately lubricated. Lack of lubrication can increase friction and lead to binding. Follow the manufacturer's recommendations for lubrication intervals and methods.

Material Buildup:Look for the accumulation of debris, product residue, or material buildup on the chain. Such buildup can create uneven surfaces and cause binding. Regularly clean the chain to prevent this issue.

Chain Condition:Examine the overall condition of the conveyor chain, including its links, plates, and attachments. Any damaged or deformed components can contribute to binding and should be replaced.

Operational Testing:Run the conveyor system through its normal operating cycles and monitor for any irregularities, such as sudden stops, jerking, or unusual noises. These can be indicative of binding issues.

Thermal Expansion:In applications with significant temperature fluctuations, consider the effects of thermal expansion and contraction on the conveyor chain. Expansion and contraction can lead to binding if not properly accounted for in the system design.

If, during any of these tests or inspections, you discover binding issues or suspect that there may be a problem, it's essential to address the root cause promptly. Correcting binding issues can prevent damage, reduce downtime, and improve the overall performance and reliability of the conveyor system.

Share

SAFOL range of NSF Certified Lubricants: India’s #1 Solution for Food & Pharmaceutical Industries

4 min read | 20 April 2023

In the food and pharmaceutical industries, ensuring product safety and regulatory compliance is critical. The right choice of food-grade lubricants plays a key role in maintaining these standards, protecting both the integrity of the products and the efficiency of the machinery.

Why Choose SAFOL NSF-Certified Lubricants?

Molygraph’s SAFOL range of food-grade lubricantsis meticulously engineered to meet the demanding requirements of the food and beverage industry. With hygiene and safety at the forefront, these lubricants are not only safe for incidental contact with food but also comply with rigorous regulatory standards set by global authorities such as theU.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA)andNSF International.

Superior Ingredients for Optimal Performance

The effectiveness of the SAFOL range is rooted in the quality of its ingredients. Our lubricants utilize premium base oils, including white mineral oils, synthetic hydrocarbons, and synthetic esters. Enhanced with food-compatible thickeners and additives, these lubricants deliver superior Extreme Pressure (EP) and Anti-Wear properties, alongside excellent corrosion prevention and oxidation stability. This ensures the longevity and efficiency of critical equipment like bearings, conveyors, chains, and gears.

Applications of SAFOL Lubricants in Food & Pharmaceutical Industries

Food Processing Equipment:

Conveyor Belts:SAFOL’s NSF-certified lubricants ensure smooth operation of conveyor systems, preventing contamination during food transportation.

Mixers and Blenders:Lubricants are essential for maintaining the efficiency of mixers and blenders, reducing wear and tear while safeguarding product purity.

Slicers and Dicers:By minimizing friction on cutting blades, SAFOL lubricants enable precise, clean cuts without compromising food safety.

Ovens and Baking Equipment:Lubricants in ovens and baking equipment help maintain consistent temperature control and prevent contamination of baked goods.

Pharmaceutical Manufacturing Equipment:

Tablet Presses and Encapsulation Machines:NSF-certified SAFOL lubricants reduce friction and wear in pharmaceutical equipment, ensuring consistent quality in medication production.

Filling and Capping Machines:Applied to the moving parts of filling and capping machines, SAFOL lubricants prevent contamination, maintaining the integrity of pharmaceutical products.

Ensuring Safety and Efficiency with SAFOL

In industries where safety, hygiene, and regulatory compliance are non-negotiable, the SAFOL range of NSF-certified lubricants stands out as the trusted choice for food processing and pharmaceutical manufacturing. By choosing Molygraph’s SAFOL lubricants, you’re not only protecting your equipment but also ensuring the safety and quality of your products.

Contact ustoday to learn how SAFOL lubricants can enhance your food and pharma operations!

pages.title: A Guide to Grease Compatibility - Know about Lubricants, Oils and Greases
pages.text:
A Guide to Grease Compatibility - Know about Lubricants, Oils and Greases

BLOG

Grease Compatibility - More critical than you think!

4 min read | 20 April 2023

Lubricating grease compatibility is crucial in industrial and automotive applications because mixing incompatible greases can lead to numerous problems affecting equipment performance, reliability, and lifespan. Here are several reasons why lubricating grease compatibility is of paramount importance:

1. Maintain Lubrication Effectiveness:

Proper Lubrication:Compatible greases ensure that the lubrication film is maintained, reducing friction and wear between moving parts.

Prevent Premature Breakdown:Incompatible greases may react adversely, leading to a breakdown of the lubrication film, causing increased friction, wear, and potential equipment failure.

Avoidance of Physical and Chemical Reactions:

Physical Reactions:Incompatible greases may exhibit physical reactions, like separation of the oil and thickener, resulting in leakage and insufficient lubrication.

Chemical Reactions:Chemical reactions between incompatible greases can lead to the formation of deposits, corrosive by-products, and a breakdown of the grease’s structure, impacting performance.

Ensure Mechanical Stability:

Maintain Consistency:Mixing incompatible greases can alter the consistency of the grease, making it either too hard or too soft, impacting its ability to stay in place and provide effective lubrication.

Prevent Bleeding:Compatibility is essential to prevent excessive oil bleeding, ensuring that the grease maintains its structure and lubricating properties under operating conditions.

Preservation of Equipment Integrity:

Prevent Corrosion:Incompatibility can lead to corrosive by-products, damaging metal surfaces and leading to premature equipment wear and failure.

Extend Equipment Life:Using compatible greases ensures proper lubrication and protection, extending the lifespan of machinery and reducing downtime and maintenance costs.

Economic and Operational Considerations:

Reduce Maintenance Costs:Ensuring compatibility prevents lubrication-related failures, reducing maintenance requirements and associated costs.

Optimize Operational Efficiency:Compatible greases maintain their lubricating properties, optimising the performance and reliability of equipment, and preventing unscheduled operational interruptions.

Steps to Ensure Compatibility:

Compatibility Testing:Before mixing different greases, perform compatibility tests to determine any adverse reactions between them. (ASTM D6185-11)

Follow Manufacturer Recommendations:Adhere to the equipment manufacturer’s recommendations on the type and specification of grease to be used.

Cleanliness:When switching greases, clean the component thoroughly to remove as much of the old grease as possible before applying the new one.

Proactive Monitoring:Regularly inspect and monitor the lubricated components for signs of incompatibility such as changes in consistency, color, or the presence of deposits.

Conclusion:

The compatibility of lubricating greases is essential in maintaining the reliability, performance, and lifespan of equipment. Mixing incompatible greases can result in detrimental physical and chemical reactions, affecting the grease’s performance, causing equipment damage, and leading to increased operational and maintenance costs. Therefore, proper measures like compatibility testing and following manufacturer recommendations should be taken to ensure the use of compatible greases in machinery and equipment.

Share

pages.title: Guidelines for selecting Gearbox Oils | Molygraph
pages.text:
Guidelines for selecting Gearbox Oils | Molygraph

BLOG

Guidelines for selecting Gearbox Oils

6 min read | 20 April 2023

Ideally, it is best practice to inspect and analyze an equipment on-site before recommending any lubricant. Many manufacturers provide guidelines for lubricant selection but fail to look into some crucial elements. The maximum operational reliability of aGearboxis not only dependent on operating materials but choosing the right lubricants during all design phases as integral structural elements.

Factors that play an important role in selection:

Gearbox type:Dimensions, the metal used for teeth, sliding properties and gear ratio, open or closed casing

Operating conditions:Depending on the load, vibration and shock levels, anti-wear and extreme pressure properties

Service oil temperature:Very low, moderate (+20°C and +80°C), hot (+80°C) and extreme (+120°C)

Machine environment and maintenance conditions

Once these factors are accounted for, the following criteria for gear oil selection come into the picture:

Viscosity– One of the most important properties of lubricating oils

Additives– This determines the category and affects specific performance properties under operating conditions

Base Oil– Determines the overall operating conditions, gear type and other factors.

VISCOSITY:

This factor is of utmost importance as it determines the formation of the lubricant film. It determines the film thickness between interacting surfaces at a given speed and load. It is difficult to ascertain the ‘load’ for most viscosity selection methods. Hence the ‘load’ is assumed, and ‘speed’ becomes the determining factor.

The degree to which viscosity changes with temperature depends on the base oil, such as mineral oil, polyalphaolefin, ester, polyglycol, as well as on the VI improver additives contained in the lubricant.

One of the most common methods for determining viscosity is the ANSI (American National Standards Institute) and AGMA (American Gear Manufacturers Association) standard ANSI/AGMA 9005-E02. Load, viscosity index and the pressure-viscosity coefficient of the lubricant are the factors used in this method.

ADDITIVES:

While there are many variations, gear lubricants fall into three general categories: R & O, antiscuff and compounded. Operating conditions determine the choice of additives in gear oils.

R&O stands for Rust and Oxidation. Anti-corrosion properties of gear oils are assessed individually for corrosion protection on steel and copper.

The presence of water due to leakage or condensation can lead to combination with ambient oxygen resulting in rust on inadequately protected steel surfaces. Additives that contain polar rust inhibitors form a compact and protective, water-repelling layer. R&O gear oils generally perform well in the categories of chemical stability, corrosion prevention and foam suppression.

Antiscuff; commonly referred to as extreme pressure (EP) lubricants have some performance capabilities that exceed those for R&O oils. In addition to the properties listed for R&O lubricants, antiscuff lubricants contain special additives that enhance their film strength or load-carrying ability.

Compounded; a compounded lubricant is mixed with synthetic fatty acids to increase its lubricity and film strength. The most common application for these gear lubricants is worm gear applications.

BASE OIL:

The quality of the base oil is a crucial factor in determining the performance of your gear oil. High-quality mineral base oils perform well in most applications. Mineral-based oils typically have higher pressure-viscosity coefficients than common synthetics, allowing for greater film thickness at given operating viscosities. There are, however, situations where synthetic base oils are preferable. Gear lubricants based on these synthetic oils have proven to be most effective: polyalphaolefin (PAO), polyglycol (PG) and ester (E)

Are you wondering what to choose between Synthetic or Mineral base oils? Stay tuned for our next blog where we break down this question.

Click Hereto see all our complete range of Gear Oils, orClick Hereto download our brochure.

Share

pages.title: Calcium Sulfonate Greases – a preferred choice across industries | Molygraph
pages.text:
Calcium Sulfonate Greases – a preferred choice across industries | Molygraph

BLOG

Calcium Sulfonate Greases – a preferred choice across industries

6 min read | 20 April 2023

Calcium Sulfonate Greases (CaS Greases) emerged in the lubrication industry as high-performance greases known for their exceptional mechanical stability, corrosion protection, high load-carrying capacity, and water resistance.

Brief History:

1960s Origin:Calcium sulfonate greases were developed in the 1960s, stemming from the technology of overbased calcium sulfonate detergents in motor oils. These greases were developed to overcome the limitations of traditional soap-based greases, offering superior performance under extreme conditions.

Technological Advancements:Over time, continuous research and developments have led to improved formulations, allowing calcium sulfonate greases to meet a variety of industrial requirements, especially in marine, mining, and steel production, where extreme pressures, high temperatures, and corrosive conditions are prevalent.

Performance Chemistry:

Corrosion Protection:The excess base (from overbasing) in the calcium sulfonate complex can neutralize acids formed during operation, providing excellent corrosion protection to metal surfaces.

High-Temperature Stability:The calcium sulfonate complex has a high dropping point, enabling the grease to maintain its structure and provide effective lubrication at elevated temperatures.

Water Resistance:The chemical structure of calcium sulfonate complex imparts excellent water resistance, preventing washout and maintaining effective lubrication in wet conditions.

Molecular Interaction:

The interaction between the calcium sulfonate molecules and the base oil creates a stable matrix that holds the oil in place.

The colloidal calcium carbonate particles within the overbased calcium sulfonate complex create a barrier, providing excellent anti-wear properties and protecting surfaces from corrosion and extreme pressures.

Today with rising costs of lithium, calcium sulfonate grease technology has once again come to the fore, with lubricant manufacturers optimising the technology to develop a multipurpose grease that can be used across a wide spectrum of heavy manufacturing industries.

Automotive Industry:

Calcium sulfonate greases are extensively used for lubricating various automotive components such as wheel bearings, chassis parts, universal joints, and other moving parts.

They are especially suitable for heavy-duty vehicles that operate under high pressure and severe conditions, like trucks and buses.

Marine Industry:

Their excellent water resistance and corrosion protection make them ideal for marine applications.

They are used to lubricate components exposed to saltwater, such as ship propeller shafts, rudder posts, and winches.

Steel and Metal Industry:

In steel mills and metalworking industries, calcium sulfonate greases are used for lubricating rollers, bearings, conveyors, and other machinery operating under high temperatures and loads.

They are also used to protect equipment from corrosion and to extend equipment lifespan.

Mining Industry:

They are utilized in various mining equipment like excavators, loaders, and drills due to their high load-carrying ability and resistance to water washout.

They help in reducing wear and tear and in extending the life of the machinery in harsh mining conditions.

Construction Industry:

Construction equipment like bulldozers, cranes, and excavators benefit from the use of calcium sulfonate greases due to their extreme pressure properties and mechanical stability.

They help protect equipment that is exposed to dust, dirt, and adverse weather conditions.

Agricultural Industry:

Agricultural machinery such as tractors, harvesters, and plows use calcium sulfonate greases for lubrication and protection against corrosion.

They are chosen for their ability to perform well in outdoor conditions and resist water washout.

Food and Beverage Industry:

Certain food-grade calcium sulfonate greases are used in food processing equipment to reduce wear and corrosion while being safe for incidental food contact.

They help in maintaining hygiene and ensuring smooth operation of the machinery.

Power Generation Industry:

Calcium sulfonate greases are used in power plants for lubricating bearings in motors, turbines, and generators.

They are preferred for their ability to withstand high temperatures and resist oxidation.

Paper Industry:

In the paper and pulp industry, they are used to lubricate and protect machinery such as paper rollers and pulpers from corrosion and wear.

Their resistance to water and high-temperature stability are crucial in this industry.

Share

Calcium Sulfonate Greases (CaS Greases) emerged in the lubrication industry as high-performance greases known for their exceptional mechanical stability, corrosion protection, high load-carrying capacity, and water resistance.

Brief History:

1960s Origin:Calcium sulfonate greases were developed in the 1960s, stemming from the technology of overbased calcium sulfonate detergents in motor oils. These greases were developed to overcome the limitations of traditional soap-based greases, offering superior performance under extreme conditions.

Technological Advancements:Over time, continuous research and developments have led to improved formulations, allowing calcium sulfonate greases to meet a variety of industrial requirements, especially in marine, mining, and steel production, where extreme pressures, high temperatures, and corrosive conditions are prevalent.

Performance Chemistry:

Corrosion Protection:The excess base (from overbasing) in the calcium sulfonate complex can neutralize acids formed during operation, providing excellent corrosion protection to metal surfaces.

High-Temperature Stability:The calcium sulfonate complex has a high dropping point, enabling the grease to maintain its structure and provide effective lubrication at elevated temperatures.

Water Resistance:The chemical structure of calcium sulfonate complex imparts excellent water resistance, preventing washout and maintaining effective lubrication in wet conditions.

Molecular Interaction:

The interaction between the calcium sulfonate molecules and the base oil creates a stable matrix that holds the oil in place.

The colloidal calcium carbonate particles within the overbased calcium sulfonate complex create a barrier, providing excellent anti-wear properties and protecting surfaces from corrosion and extreme pressures.

Today with rising costs of lithium, calcium sulfonate grease technology has once again come to the fore, with lubricant manufacturers optimising the technology to develop a multipurpose grease that can be used across a wide spectrum of heavy manufacturing industries.

Automotive Industry:

Calcium sulfonate greases are extensively used for lubricating various automotive components such as wheel bearings, chassis parts, universal joints, and other moving parts.

They are especially suitable for heavy-duty vehicles that operate under high pressure and severe conditions, like trucks and buses.

Marine Industry:

Their excellent water resistance and corrosion protection make them ideal for marine applications.

They are used to lubricate components exposed to saltwater, such as ship propeller shafts, rudder posts, and winches.

Steel and Metal Industry:

In steel mills and metalworking industries, calcium sulfonate greases are used for lubricating rollers, bearings, conveyors, and other machinery operating under high temperatures and loads.

They are also used to protect equipment from corrosion and to extend equipment lifespan.

Mining Industry:

They are utilized in various mining equipment like excavators, loaders, and drills due to their high load-carrying ability and resistance to water washout.

They help in reducing wear and tear and in extending the life of the machinery in harsh mining conditions.

Construction Industry:

Construction equipment like bulldozers, cranes, and excavators benefit from the use of calcium sulfonate greases due to their extreme pressure properties and mechanical stability.

They help protect equipment that is exposed to dust, dirt, and adverse weather conditions.

Agricultural Industry:

Agricultural machinery such as tractors, harvesters, and plows use calcium sulfonate greases for lubrication and protection against corrosion.

They are chosen for their ability to perform well in outdoor conditions and resist water washout.

Food and Beverage Industry:

Certain food-grade calcium sulfonate greases are used in food processing equipment to reduce wear and corrosion while being safe for incidental food contact.

They help in maintaining hygiene and ensuring smooth operation of the machinery.

Power Generation Industry:

Calcium sulfonate greases are used in power plants for lubricating bearings in motors, turbines, and generators.

They are preferred for their ability to withstand high temperatures and resist oxidation.

Paper Industry:

In the paper and pulp industry, they are used to lubricate and protect machinery such as paper rollers and pulpers from corrosion and wear.

Their resistance to water and high-temperature stability are crucial in this industry.

pages.title: Right Selection of Base oils - Know about Lubricants, Oils and Greases
pages.text:
Right Selection of Base oils - Know about Lubricants, Oils and Greases

BLOG

Right Selection of Base oils

9 min read | 19 April 2023

Introduction:

Selecting the right base oil is crucial in formulating lubricants and greases. Several factors play an important role in the selection criteria. Specific recommendations regarding the base oil choice can be provided, but still, those recommendations can be completely different from the real environmental conditions. Lubricant manufacturers and users must develop a deep understanding of the application before selecting the base oil. Although additives are being used to enhance the performance of lubricants, the base oils’ performance properties are often undermined or ignored entirely. Lubricants and Greases are composed of base oils, thickeners (in greases) & additives. The base oils are a major part of these systems, occupying more than 80% composition. It is now well researched and concluded that though additives enhance many properties of base oils such as Extreme Pressure, Antiwear, and Oxidation Stability, some important properties such as Thermal Stability, Hydrolytic Stability, and Seal Compatibility are inherent properties of base oils that are not touched by additives in any means. Further, the response and solubility of additives are heavily dependent on the base oil chemistry and viscosity. In this paper, the technical team from Molygraph Lubricants explains the properties of different base oils. According to the American Petroleum Institute (API), the base oil is classified into five groups. The first three groups (Group I, II, and III) are refined from petroleum crude oil. Group IV base oils are full synthetic (Polyalphaolefin) oils. Group V is for all other base oils not included in Groups I through IV.

Mineral Oils

Group-I base oils are manufactured by the solvent extraction refining process. It comprises three primary molecule types: paraffinic, naphthenic, and aromatic, along with varieties of sulfurnitrogen-based compounds. It contains large amounts of unsaturated molecules than any other base oil type. These unsaturated molecules are responsible for accelerating the aging and degradation of finished lubricant products. Therefore, Group I mineral oils are regarded as poor in oxidative and thermal stability. However, it is a result of these unsaturated compounds due to which Group I base oils show the highest degree of additive solvency than any other base oils. Most lubricant formulators avoid using Group I Mineral oil for formulating lubricants requiring longer drain or re-lubrication intervals and/ or higher operating temperatures. However, for formulations where a high amount of polar additives are required, such as Rust Preventives or Neat Metal working fluids, Group I base oils are preferred. Group II and Group III are preferred for Engine oils, Hydraulic oils, compressor oils, Turbine oils, Greases, and are becoming dominant over the Group I base oil. Group-II base oils are manufactured similar to Group-I base oil but with a hydrocracked process. In this process, hydrogen reacts with aromatic and naphthenic molecules, and the amount of unsaturated molecules is less than the Group -I. Group III base oils follow the same hydrogen processing path as the GIIs, except they are more severely treated (higher pressure, higher temperature, longer process times), reducing unsaturated compounds nearly to zero. The hydrocracking and hydroprocessing add oxidation and thermal stability to Group II and Group III-based Mineral oils making them preferable for most Automotive and Industrial Lubricants and Greases.

Poly Alpha Olefines

Group-IV base oil consists of only Polyalphaolefins (also known as PAO). These are synthetically made by polymerizing α-olefins. The process ensures no unsaturation and aromatics in the yielded base oil, imparting excellent oxidation and thermal stability. However, this also results in poor additive response and seal compatibility. To take care of these issues, Esters are added to a certain concentration into the PAOs. PAOs are hydrolytically very stable due to the absence of a chemical functional group that could hydrolyze in the presence of water and elevated temperatures.

Esters

Ester is an example of group V base oils. Esters are synthesized by reacting Acid and Alcohol. Complex esters can be synthesized by adding some specialized production techniques. Esters have high polarity, which enhances additive solubility and seals compatibility in PAO-based lubricants and greases caused by a low polar nature of PAOs. Often, esters are added as co-base oils with PAOs or Mineral oils. However, some applications demand Esters be used as the only base oil in the formulation, such as Refrigeration compressor oils, High-temperature Chain oils used in Paint curing ovens, and Textile Stenters due to their high flashpoints, low pour points, lubricity, and low evaporation rates. Esters are generally hydrolytically unstable due to –COOR functional group which hydrolyzes quickly. However, some esters are made through advanced processes that improve hydrolytic stability.

Polyalkylene Glycols

Polyalkylene Glycols are synthetic base oils included in the group V category by API. There are three types of PAGs available commercially: water-soluble, water-insoluble, and Oil Soluble. Machinery that are using petroleum lubricants have wear grooves with carbonaceous material. Good solvency tendency of PAG helps to remove all the carbonaceous and thus revealing the wear scars which were earlier invisible. PAGs are hygroscopic but the water absorbed by the PAG is not free but is instead bound to the backbone of PAG. Thus, neither the corrosion nor the ice-crystal formation has been a problem when lubricating with PAG based lubricants.

Silicone Oil

Silicone base oils are linear polysiloxane compounds. They are chemically inert, thermo oxidatively stable and hydrolytically stable. Due to these properties silicone oil is used as a sealant. They have extremely high shear resistance compared to mineral oil.

Perfluoroalkylpolyethers (PFPE)

PFPE base oil are used as lubricants in oxygen services because they are chemically inert, thermo oxidatively stable and non-flammable. They can be used as high temperature greases because of their ability to withstand constant temperature upto 288°C.They are mainly used in vacuum pumps.

Conclusion:

Knowing the characteristics of the base oil will help to choose the right base oil according to the application. Each base oil type serves different properties and advantages. Therefore, in some application combinations of base oils are preferred.

Share

pages.title: https://www.molygraph.com/newsroom/why-polyurea-greases-are-ideal-for-electric-motor-bearings
pages.text:
BLOG

Why Polyurea Greases Are Ideal for Electric Motor Bearings

4 min read | 20 April 2023

When it comes to ensuring the smooth and long-lasting performance of electric fan motors,lubricationplays a critical role. One of the most reliable choices forelectric fan motor bearingsispolyurea grease, and for good reason. Known for its superior thermal stability, extended service life, and exceptional resistance to oxidation and moisture, polyurea grease is fast becoming the go-to solution in industrial and domestic  motor applications.

Key Benefits of Using Polyurea Grease in Motor Bearings

1. High-Temperature Stability

While electric  motors may not generate extreme heat, they do operate continuously. Polyurea greases perform reliably across a wide temperature range, making them suitable for varying operational environments. This temperature resilience helps maintain grease integrity over time.

2. Extended Service Life

One of the standout features of polyurea greases is their long operational life. Unlike conventional greases, they do not break down easily, significantly reducing the need for frequent re-lubrication and lowering overall maintenance costs.

3. Superior Oxidation Resistance

Oxidation can lead to the formation of deposits and sludge that compromise bearing performance. Polyurea grease resists oxidation effectively, maintaining its lubricating properties and ensuring the long-term protection of motor components.

4. Reduced Noise and Vibration

Proper bearing lubrication using polyurea greases contributes to quieter, smoother motor operation. This is especially important in applications where low noise levels are a priority, such as in HVAC systems or consumer appliances.

5. Excellent Moisture and Water Washout Resistance

In environments where humidity or direct exposure to moisture is a concern, polyurea greases stand out for their strong resistance to water washout. This property makes them particularly effective for motors to operate in  coastal locations.

Best Practices for Application of Polyurea Grease

To get the most from polyurea greases, proper application is essential. Here’s how they’re typically used in electric  motor bearings:

Initial Lubrication:Bearings are usually lubricated during assembly using the recommended quantity of polyurea grease. Over-greasing should be avoided to prevent excess pressure and heat buildup.

Re-lubrication:Although polyurea greases boast a long service life, routine inspections are advisable — especially for motors that operate continuously or under demanding conditions. Re-lubricate only as needed.

Ongoing Maintenance:Regular cleaning and monitoring of bearing condition are crucial. This ensures contaminants are kept out, and the grease maintains its performance characteristics.

If you're looking for a reliable, long-lasting lubrication solution for electric motors,polyurea greaseis a top choice. Itshigh-temperature stability,oxidation resistance, andextended service lifemake it ideal for ensuring optimal bearing performance, reducing maintenance needs, and enhancing overall fan motor efficiency.

For industries or applications demanding consistent performance and durability, investing in the right polyurea grease—and applying it correctly—can make all the difference.

pages.title: Understanding Lubricating Greases - Molygraph Lubrications
pages.text:
Understanding Lubricating Greases - Molygraph Lubrications

BLOG

Understanding Lubricating Greases

5 min read | 20 April 2023

Machines are essential to industries, and friction is an unavoidable challenge that can impact their performance. While friction is beneficial in many daily applications, it causes wear and tear in machines. This is where lubricants, particularly lubricating greases, play a vital role. In industrial applications, where machinery operates under high stress, extreme temperatures, and fluctuating conditions, lubricating greases are crucial in reducing friction, wear, and tear, ultimately extending the lifespan of the equipment.

What are Lubricating Greases?

The American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) defines lubricating grease as "a solid to semifluid product of dispersion of a thickening agent in a liquid lubricant. Other ingredients imparting special properties may be included" (ASTM D 288).

In simpler terms, lubricating greases are a mixture of oils, thickeners, and additives designed to reduce friction in machinery, ensuring smoother operations under challenging conditions such as exposure to water, dust, and high temperatures. By minimizing wear, greases increase the machine’s lifespan and efficiency.

Composition of Lubricating Greases

Lubricating greases consist of three main components:

75% Base Oil: Responsible for actual lubrication.

15% Thickener: Provides the grease's consistency.

10% Additives: Enhance the grease's performance and durability.

Each component plays a vital role in determining how effective the grease is in different applications.

Base Oils in Lubricating Greases

The base oil is the primary lubricant in the grease, and its viscosity plays a crucial role in determining the grease's performance. Commonly used base oils include:

Mineral Oils: Widely used in industrial greases.

Synthetic Oils (PAO, Esters): Known for superior thermal stability and performance under extreme temperatures.

Biobased Oils: Gaining popularity due to their eco-friendly properties.

High-viscosity oils are preferred for heavy loads and slow speeds, while low-viscosity oils work well in high-speed applications. Selecting the right base oil is critical for optimizing machinery performance.

Thickening Agents in Lubricating Greases

The thickener provides the grease with its semi-solid structure. It is essential to choose the right thickener for specific applications based on factors like temperature, load, and water resistance. Common thickeners include:

Metallic Soaps: Lithium, calcium, sodium, and aluminum.

Non-Soap Thickeners: Clay, polyurea.

A grease's consistency is crucial for effective lubrication. Grease that is too stiff may not reach all areas requiring lubrication, while grease that is too fluid may leak.

Additives in Lubricating Greases

Additives are critical for enhancing the performance of lubricating greases. Though they make up only a small percentage of the formulation, they play a significant role in boosting desirable properties while suppressing undesirable ones. Common additives include:

Oxidation and Rust Inhibitors: Prevent corrosion and degradation of the grease.

Extreme Pressure (EP) Additives: Enhance load-carrying capacity.

Anti-Wear Agents: Minimize wear on metal surfaces.

Friction Reducers: Reduce friction, allowing smoother machine operations.

By incorporating these additives, lubricating greases ensure machinery performs optimally under demanding conditions, increasing both productivity and equipment lifespan.

Applications of Lubricating Greases Across Industries

Lubricating greases have broad applications across several industries, including:

Food and Pharma: Ensuring equipment runs smoothly in clean environments.

Iron and Steel: Withstanding extreme temperatures and pressures.

Mining: Handling heavy-duty operations and exposure to harsh environments.

Oil and Gas: Ensuring efficient operation in high-temperature and high-pressure conditions.

Why Lubricating Greases Matter?

In any industrial setup, selecting the right lubricating grease is essential to maximize machinery performance and minimize downtime. High-quality lubricating greases reduce maintenance costs and extend the lifespan of machinery. With the right formulation of base oils, thickeners, and additives, industries can achieve higher efficiency and productivity.

Explore our Lubricating Greases

Contact Us

Share

pages.title: Hot & Cold Metal Forging: Now Extra Efficient - Know about Lubricants, Oils and Greases
pages.text:
Hot & Cold Metal Forging: Now Extra Efficient - Know about Lubricants, Oils and Greases

BLOG

Hot & Cold Metal Forging: Now Extra Efficient

3 min read | 20 April 2023

Molygraph’s Graphol range ofForging Lubricantsis a complete solution whether your application is Hot, Warm, or Cold forging. They are designed to operate under extreme temperatures and pressure providing a superior coating on the billet and die, minimizes metal to metal contact, and enhances productivity and die life. Molygraph’s, Graphol, AF, And AS series are available in various variants and take care of all your forging needs.

Hot Forging Lubricants

A series of lubricant products, namely the Graphol and AF series has been developed over years of partnered projects and R&D. Its optimised particle size and affordable cost to benefit ratio has successfully satisfied our clientele needs.

The lubricant contains special binders and polymers, providing excellent film formation and metal flow. Any stickiness, pitting and under-filling are eliminated as a result of this product.

TheGraphol Series Of Lubricantscontain water, graphite/oil, graphite/water, and polymer-based formulations.

BLOG

SMG Premium – The Ultimate Lubrication Solution for Sugar Mills

5 min read | 20 April 2023

India ranks among the top consumers and producers of sugar globally, making efficient and uninterrupted production in sugar mills absolutely vital. However, these mills operate under extreme mechanical and environmental stress, where inadequate lubrication can lead to costly downtime, increased wear, and frequent maintenance.

SMG Premium– a high-performance, specially engineered grease designed byMolygraphto address the unique lubrication challenges of the sugar industry.

Why SMG Premium is the Ideal Choice for Sugar Mill Operations

Backed by over35 years of innovation and R&D, Molygraph’s SMG Premium is part of a trusted range of industrial lubricants. This formulation is crafted to enhance the performance of Mill equipment supplemented with advanced tribological testings to counter the lubrication related challenges.

Key Operational Challenges in Sugar Mills—and How SMG Premium Solves Them

1. High Pressure and Inadequate Lubrication

Sugarcane juice extractors operate under heavy load and pressure, slowing down processing time and increasing energy loss.SMG Premiumis formulated withhigh base oil viscosityand fortified withEP (Extreme Pressure)andAW (Anti-Wear)additives. This combination ensures:

Better load-bearing capacity

Reduced friction and power losses

A strong lubricating film that protects components even under high stress

2. Cracks and Burn Marks on Bearings

Shock loads are common during mill operations and can cause significant bearing damage.SMG Premiumcontains ahigh concentration of solid lubricants, delivering:

Emergency lubrication during shock load surges

Prevention of surface cracks and heat-induced burn marks

Extended component life in demanding environments

3. Grease Washout and Acid Corrosion

Sugarcane processing generates acidic by-products and moisture, causing grease washout and corrosion.SMG Premium includes:

A specializedwater- and acid-resistant thickener

Advanced additives that combat corrosion effectively

Long-lasting protection in humid and acidic conditions

4. Choking of Grease Lines and Air Locks

Traditional greases can clog filters and lines, disrupting the entire lubrication system.SMG Premium’scomplex soap structureensures:

Smooth flow in automated grease pumping systems

No choking or air-lock formation

Consistent and continuous lubrication to all vital components

Our Expert Support—Every Step of the Way

At Molygraph, we don’t just deliver a product—we deliver a complete lubrication solution. Our experiencedteam of engineersworks directly with sugar mill operators to:

Troubleshoot application issues

Optimize lubricant usage

Reduce unplanned maintenance and downtime

We are committed to helping you achievehigher equipment reliability, extended service intervals, and cost savings.

Why Choose Molygraph?

With decades of expertise in specialty lubricants and a deep understanding of the sugar industry,Molygraphhas built a reputation for deliveringhigh-performance, cost-effective lubrication solutions. We focus on long-term partnerships, product customization, and technical excellence.

Download the SMG Premium Brochureto learn more about our product’s technical details and applications.Connect with our lubrication expertstoday to improve your plant’s performance and reduce maintenance costs.

🔗Explore SMG Premium on our website

📧 sales@molygraph.com | 📞 +91 8104409925

pages.title: Wire Rope Lubricants: Introducing Quality Enhancements - Know about Lubricants, Oils and Greases
pages.text:
Wire Rope Lubricants: Introducing Quality Enhancements - Know about Lubricants, Oils and Greases

BLOG

Wire Rope Lubricants: Introducing Quality Enhancements

3 min read | 20 April 2023

Metal wire ropes undergo periodic wear rates over time. Industries that use wire ropes are well aware of its influencing factors. The extent of metal wearing off depends on the environment in which the rope is used, and the method of operation. Improper handling, storing, and high-stress loading are seemingly other factors that lead to corrosion.

Molygraph has therefore upgraded two of its lubricant productsCRG 100/MandCRG 100/M Fluid.The upgrade speaks of its enhanced properties that help extend the lifecycle quality of wire ropes and their use.

Let’s browse in detail on the two upgraded products.

CRG 100/M

This lubricant is a coating compound used in transmission, anchors, rollers, and sling chains.

It has low penetrating properties but has good adhesive coating characteristics, fortified with rust and corrosion inhibitors. Hence, this property aids in the added protection of wire ropes and cables exposed to salty seawater.

Other applications include their use for crane cables, elevator chains, chain drive sprockets, mobile or forklifts, multiple strand ropes, Warrington ropes, wire ropes for ropeways, and overhead cranes.

Additional premium upgrades and features of CRG 100/M

Eco-friendly

Contains no harmful ingredients or corrosion-causing chemicals.

Temperature-resistant

Withstands temperature changes and rubbing-off action.

Fluid consistency

For easy brush ability, dipping, and application.

Adherence

Adheres well to strands and reduces friction between strands, especially when the rope bends over sheaves or drums.

Penetrative

Penetrates efficiently and lubricates effectively at the same time, forming a protective coating.

Rust/Corrosion-resistant

Contains dewatering properties and resists water-washout; thus, offering protection against rust and corrosion.

Friction-reduced

Is fortified with Graphite, MoS2, and other chosen additives that reduce internal friction and wear rate.

Noiseless

Enablesoperational functioning without any extreme noise of the chains in use.

Extreme endurance

Provides excellent protection from environments such as the sun, heat, saltwater, humid weather, and aggressive chemicals.

CRG 100/M Fluid

This is a penetrating fluid that aids in applications including rope cables in anchors, roller and sling chains, fixed or mobile cranes, and dragline hoists.

This product contains a high percentage of solvent in its formulation, enabling deep penetration of grease into the strands. The fluid lubricates the strands, and central hemp or the core wire, thereby, extending its lifecycle by preventing frequent premature failures of costly wire ropes.

Grease applications are used at seaports and terminals, airports, heavy engineering workshops, warehouses, and mines.

Additional premium upgrades and features of CRG 100/M Fluid

Temperature-resistant

Adheres well to strands to withstand temperature changes and rubbing-off action.

Eco-friendly

Unlike cheap tar-based lubricants, this fluid does not contain corrosive or harmful ingredients.

Rust/Corrosion-resistant

Excellent dewatering properties; resisting grease water-washout to protect against rust and corrosion.

Friction-reduced

Ingredients such as MoS2 and Graphite are used as solid lubricants to help reduce friction and metal wear.

No-drip grease

Avoids messy floors or surroundings as the grease does not drip even at elevated temperatures.

Salt water-friendly

Enhanced corrosion resistant properties against salty seawater.

Fluid consistency

Easy brush ability, dipping, and spraying application.

With these enhanced properties, industrial applications can certainly function well within hassle-free operations. Molygraph proves to be the prime choice for performance with the continued enhancement of lubricant products for several industries.

WhileOur Expertsare available to discuss more details, you may browse through a range ofProductson our website. We have innovative and technically engineered products used in various industries such as oil & gas to automotive, mining, steel, metal forging, and more.

Share
